title: EXPOSÉ XII NIELSEN-WECKEN AND LEFSCHETZ FORMULAS IN ALGEBRAIC GEOMETRY

pages.title: EXPOSÉ XII NIELSEN-WECKEN AND LEFSCHETZ FORMULAS IN ALGEBRAIC GEOMETRY

blocks.html:
EXPOSÉ XII
NIELSEN-WECKEN AND LEFSCHETZ FORMULAS IN ALGEBRAIC GEOMETRYby A. Grothendieck
written up by I. BUCUR
Let \(X'\) be a scheme proper over the algebraically closed field \(k\) and \(G\) a finite group acting on \(X'\).
For each element \(g\in G\) we denote by \({}_gX':X'\longrightarrow X'\) the automorphism of \(X'\) induced by \(g\).

Suppose that there exists an open subset \(U'\) of \(X'\) stable under \(G\) and such that \(G\) acts freely on \(U'\), and let \(\Lambda_n=\mathbb Z/\ell^n\mathbb Z\) where \(\ell\) is a prime number different from the characteristic of \(k\).

Denote by \(\Lambda_{n,U',X'}\) the sheaf \(i_!(\Lambda_{n,U'})\) on \(X'\), where \(\Lambda_{n,U'}\) is the constant sheaf on \(U'\) associated to the ring \(\Lambda_n\) and \(i:U'\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow X'\) is the canonical immersion of \(U'\) into \(X'\). The sheaf \(\Lambda_{n,U',X'}\) is endowed in a natural way with a structure of sheaf with group \(G\) of operators. Consequently the complex
is a complex of \(\Lambda_n[G]\)-modules, and more precisely a perfect complex (VIII,7) of \(\Lambda_n[G]\)-modules (IX,2).

We shall see later that it is more convenient for our purpose to consider, instead of the complex \(R\Gamma_{X'}(\Lambda_{n,U',X'})\), its dual \(\mathcal H_{n,U',X'}^\vee=R\Gamma_{X'}(\Lambda_{n,U',X'})^\vee\) with respect to the ring \(\Lambda_n\) in the sense of derived categories:
The complex \(R\Gamma_{X'}(\Lambda_{n,U',X'})^\vee\) is a perfect complex of \(\Lambda_n[G]\)-modules.

Suppose now that we are given an endomorphism \(f':X'\longrightarrow X'\) of the scheme \(X'\) and an endomorphism \(\varphi:G\longrightarrow G\) of the group \(G\) such that the following two conditions are satisfied:
(Under the hypotheses already made, \(Y'\) is stable under \(G\). In general we do not require that the open set \(U'\) be stable under \(f'\)).
We thus obtain the homomorphisms of sheaves:
such that the following diagram, in which the rows are exact, is commutative:
From this we deduce, in particular, homomorphisms of complexes of \(\Lambda_n\)-modules:

\[ f'_{\mathcal H_{n,U',X'}}=R\Gamma_{X'}(f'_{\Lambda_{n,U',X'}}): \mathcal H_{n,U',X'}\longrightarrow\mathcal H_{n,U',X'}, \] \[ f'_{\mathcal H_{n,U',X'}^\vee}=(f'_{\mathcal H_{n,U',X'}})^\vee: \mathcal H_{n,U',X'}^\vee\longrightarrow\mathcal H_{n,U',X'}^\vee, \] \[ {}_g\mathcal H_{n,U',X'}=R\Gamma_{X'}({}_g\Lambda_{n,U',X'}): \mathcal H_{n,U',X'}\longrightarrow\mathcal H_{n,U',X'}, \] \[ {}_g\mathcal H_{n,U',X'}^\vee=({}_g\mathcal H_{n,U',X'})^\vee: \mathcal H_{n,U',X'}^\vee\longrightarrow\mathcal H_{n,U',X'}^\vee, \]

\[ (1.4)\qquad {}_g\mathcal H_{n,U',X'}\circ f'_{\mathcal H_{n,U',X'}} =f'_{\mathcal H_{n,U',X'}}\circ{}_{\varphi(g)}\mathcal H_{n,U',X'}, \] \[ (1.5)\qquad f'_{\mathcal H_{n,U',X'}^\vee}\circ{}_g\mathcal H_{n,U',X'}^\vee ={}_{\varphi(g)}\mathcal H_{n,U',X'}^\vee\circ f'_{\mathcal H_{n,U',X'}^\vee}. \]
Relation (1.5) shows us (cf. Exp. XI on the general theory of traces, 5.2.1, 7.1) that the trace

Our first aim will be to give, under some supplementary hypotheses, an explicit formula for \(\operatorname{Tr}_{\Lambda_n[G],\varphi}^{*}(f'_{\mathcal H_{n,U',X'}^\vee})\) in terms of local invariants associated to \(f'\); terms which we shall define below.
In fact one can do better. For this let \(n,m\) be two positive integers such that \(m\geq n\). The ring homomorphisms:
give rise to the homomorphisms of sheaves of rings on \(X'\):
The application of the Künneth formula gives us:

\[ (2.1)\qquad R\Gamma_{X'}(\Lambda_{m,X'})\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{\Lambda_m}\Lambda_n \simeq R\Gamma_{X'}(\Lambda_{n,X'}), \] \[ (2.2)\qquad R\Gamma_{X'}(\Lambda_{m,U',X'})\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{\Lambda_m}\Lambda_n \simeq R\Gamma_{X'}(\Lambda_{n,U',X'}), \] \[ (2.3)\qquad R\Gamma_{X'}(f'_{\Lambda_{m,X'}})\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{\Lambda_m}\Lambda_n \simeq R\Gamma_{X'}(f'_{\Lambda_{n,X'}}), \] \[ (2.4)\qquad R\Gamma_{X'}(f'_{\Lambda_{m,U',X'}})\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{\Lambda_m}\Lambda_n \simeq R\Gamma_{X'}(f'_{\Lambda_{n,U',X'}}). \]

\[ (2.5)\qquad R\operatorname{Hom}_{\Lambda_m}\bigl(R\Gamma_{X'}(\Lambda_{m,U',X'}),\Lambda_m\bigr) \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{\Lambda_m}\Lambda_n \simeq R\operatorname{Hom}_{\Lambda_n}\bigl(R\Gamma_{X'}(\Lambda_{n,U',X'}),\Lambda_n\bigr) \] \[ (2.6)\qquad f'_{\mathcal H_{m,U',X'}^\vee}\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{\Lambda_m}\Lambda_n \simeq f'_{\mathcal H_{n,U',X'}^\vee}. \]

The morphism of perfect complexes \(R\Gamma_{X'}(f'_{\Lambda_{n,X'}})\) (resp. \(R\Gamma_{X'}(f'_{\Lambda_{n,U',X'}}), f'_{\mathcal H_{n,U',X'}^\vee}\)) is thus obtained by extension of scalars (XI, 6.3) from the morphisms \(R\Gamma_{X'}(f'_{\Lambda_{m,X'}})\) (resp. \(R\Gamma_{X'}(f'_{\Lambda_{m,U',X'}}), f'_{\mathcal H_{m,U',X'}^\vee}\))
The system of functions \(\bigl(\operatorname{Tr}_{\Lambda_n[G],\varphi}^{*}(f'_{\mathcal H_{n,U',X'}^\vee})\bigr)_{n>0}\) defines a function
\(\varphi\)-central on \(G\), with values in the ring \(\mathbb Z_\ell\) of \(\ell\)-adic integers.

Similarly the elements \(\operatorname{Tr}_{\Lambda_n}^{*}(R\Gamma_{X'}(f'_{\Lambda_{n,X'}}))\), \(n\geq1\), define a \(\ell\)-adic integer \(\operatorname{Tr}_{\mathbb Z_\ell}^{*}(f'_{R\Gamma_{X'}(\mathbb Z_{\ell,X'})})\).
Our aim will be to find a formula for \(\operatorname{Tr}_{\mathbb Z_\ell[G],\varphi}^{*} (f'_{\mathcal H_{U',X'}^\vee}(\ell))\).

Suppose, in addition to the hypotheses described in section 1, that every orbit of \(G\) is contained in an affine open set. There exists therefore a quotient \(X=X'/G\) and, thanks to formula 1.1, the endomorphism \(f'\) of \(X'\) passes to the quotient:
We shall use the following notations in the sequel:
\[ p:X'\longrightarrow X=X'/G\quad\text{for the canonical projection}, \] \[Y=p(Y')=Y'/G,\qquad U=X-Y.\]

\(U'\) is a principal covering having \(G\) as structural group. Let \(x\in U\) be a fixed point of \(f\). We shall associate to it a class of \(\varphi\)-conjugation (XI, 7.1) \(NW_f^{G,\varphi}(x)\) in the following manner:

Let \(x'\in U'\) be such that \(p(x')=x\). Since \(U'\) is a principal covering having \(G\) as structural group and the point \(x\) is fixed under \(f\), there exists a unique element \(g\in G\) such that \(f'(x')=gx'\). The class of \(\varphi\)-conjugation of \(g\) depends only on \(x\). Indeed if \(p(x'')=x\), there exists \(h\in G\) such that \(hx''=x'\). Consequently if \(f'(x'')=g'x''\) we have:

that is to say \(g\) and \(g'\) are \(\varphi\)-conjugate. Consequently, we obtain a well-determined class of \(\varphi\)-conjugation, the local invariant of Nielsen-Wecken associated to the fixed point \(x\in U\) of \(f\), which will be denoted by \(NW_f^{G,\varphi}(x)\).
The class of \(\varphi\)-conjugation \(NW_f^{G,\varphi}(x)\) defines a \(\varphi\)-central function (cf. XI, 7.1):
with integer values, characterized by the following relations:
\[ (3.1)\qquad NW_f^{G,\varphi}(x)(g)= \begin{cases} 1&\text{if there exists }x'\in X'\text{ such that }(g^{-1}f')(x')=x'\\ &\text{and }p(x')=x,\\ 0&\text{otherwise.} \end{cases} \]

Suppose \(X'\) smooth over \(k\) and moreover that the number of fixed points of \(f\) is finite. It follows in particular that the number of fixed points of \(g^{-1}f'\) is finite for each \(g\in G\).
For each fixed point \(x\in X\) of \(f\) we introduce the \(\varphi\)-central function
with rational values, defined by the formula:

\[ (4.2)\qquad c^\varphi(g)=\operatorname{Card}G_g^\varphi,qquad G_g^\varphi=\{x\in G\mid\varphi(x)gx^{-1}=g\}, \] \[ (4.3)\qquad X_x'^{\,g^{-1}f'}= \{x'\in X'\mid p(x')=x,\ (g^{-1}f')(x')=x'\}. \] \[ (4.4)\qquad \nu_{x'}(g^{-1}f')=\text{the multiplicity of the fixed point }x' \text{ of }g^{-1}f'. \]
the function \(S_x(f',f,G,\varphi)\) gives rise to \(\varphi\)-central functions \(S_{x,\ell}(f',f,G,\varphi)\) on \(G\) with values in the field \(\mathbb Q_\ell\) of \(\ell\)-adic numbers.

blocks.title: Conjecture 4.5.
blocks.html:
Conjecture 4.5.

For each \(\ell\ne\operatorname{car}(k)\) the values of the function \(S_{x,\ell}(f',f,G,\varphi)\) are \(\ell\)-adic integers.
We shall prove in the sequel that conjecture 4.5 is true if the dimension of \(X\) is equal to 1.

blocks.title: Proposition 4.6.
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.6.

Let \(R\) be a discrete valuation ring, \(\mathfrak m\) its maximal ideal, \(\pi\) a uniformizer, \(k=R/\mathfrak m\) its residue field, assumed alg. closed, \(v\) its valuation, \(f'_R:R\to R\) an endomorphism of \(R\) and \(G\) a finite group of automorphisms of \(R\). Suppose that the endomorphism \(f'_R\) and the automorphisms \(g_R\) induce the identity on \(k\) and that we are moreover given an endomorphism of \(G\):

\[\varphi:G\longrightarrow G,\]

such that the following relation is satisfied:

\[f'_R\circ g_R=\varphi(g)_R\circ f'_R.\]

If we denote for each \(g\in G\)

\[\nu(g_R^{-1}f'_R)=v(g_R^{-1}f'_R(\pi)-\pi),\]

and if we decompose \(c^\varphi(g)\) (cf. 4.2) in the form

\[c^\varphi(g)=p^s q,qquad(p,q)=1,qquad p=\operatorname{car}(k),\]

then \(q\mid(\nu(g_R^{-1}f'_R)-1)\).

To prove proposition 4.6 we reduce to the case \(g=e\) and \(\varphi=\operatorname{id}_G\). Indeed, we can first reduce to the case \(g=e\) by associating to the situation \((f'_R,G,g,\varphi)\) the situation \((f''_R=g_R^{-1}f'_R,G,e,\varphi'=\operatorname{int}(g)\circ\varphi)\) (\(\varphi'(a)=g^{-1}\varphi(a)g\)) and observing easily that we have:

To reduce simultaneously to the case \(g=e\), \(\varphi=\operatorname{id}_G\), let us remark that we can associate to the situation \((f''_R,G,e,\varphi')\) the situation \((f''_R,G_e^{\varphi'},e,\operatorname{id}_{G_e^{\varphi'}})\) and that we have:
The proof of proposition 4.6 relies essentially on the following lemma:

blocks.title: Lemma 4.7.
blocks.html:
Lemma 4.7.

The hypotheses and notations being those of proposition 4.6, suppose moreover that \(\varphi=\operatorname{id}_G\), that is, that the operations of \(G\) on \(R\) commute with \(f'_R\), and that \(r=\nu(f'_R)\geq2\).

Let

\[ c=\operatorname{Card}\bigl(\operatorname{Im}(G\longrightarrow \operatorname{Aut}_k(\mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2))\bigr). \]

Then we have the following divisibility relation:
Proof. It is clear that the integer \(r\) satisfies the following conditions:
On the other hand it is evident that \(G\) acts on all the vector spaces \(\mathfrak m^i/\mathfrak m^{i+1}\) over \(k\).
We shall first show that under the hypotheses of the lemma there exists a homomorphism of \(k[G]\)-modules
and only one, such that the following two conditions are satisfied:
2) \(u(f'_R)(x\bmod\mathfrak m^2)=(f'_R(x)-x)\bmod\mathfrak m^{r+1}\), for every \(x\in\mathfrak m\).
It is clear that condition a) implies \(f'_R(x)-x\in\mathfrak m^r\) for every \(x\in R\), which gives a meaning to condition 2).
This observation also gives us the possibility of defining a homomorphism of abelian groups:
This homomorphism vanishes on \(\mathfrak m^2\). Indeed, if \(x_1,x_2\in\mathfrak m\) then \(f'_R(x_1x_2)-x_1x_2\in\mathfrak m^{r+1}\) because we have, thanks to condition a):
There results therefore, by passage to the quotient, a homomorphism of abelian groups:
The homomorphism \(u(f'_R)\) is in fact a homomorphism of vector spaces over \(k\). To see this we must show that we have, for every \(x\in\mathfrak m\), \(\alpha\in R\):
\[ f'_R(\alpha)-\alpha\in\mathfrak m^r\quad\text{(Condition a))} \] \[ f'_R(x)\in\mathfrak m\qquad(x\in\mathfrak m). \]
The homomorphism \(u(f'_R)\) is a homomorphism of \(k[G]\)-modules. Indeed, we have for \(x\in\mathfrak m\):

Finally \(u(f'_R)\ne0\). Indeed, thanks to condition b) there exists an element \(x_0\in R\) such that \(f'_R(x_0)-x_0\notin\mathfrak m^{r+1}\). But we need such an element belonging to \(\mathfrak m\). To obtain such an element let us write \(x_0\) in the form
At least one of the elements \(x,x'\) satisfies the required condition.
Suppose, by contradiction, that we have at the same time:
We shall then have, in the field \(K\) of fractions of \(A\):

We may suppose that \(f'_R(x')\ne0\), because otherwise \(x'\) is the desired element. Under this hypothesis, taking into account the fact that \(1+\mathfrak m^r\) is a multiplicative group, it follows that:
If we endow the vector space over \(k\):
with the usual structure of \(G\)-module, its element \(u(f'_R)\) belongs to the subspace

On the other hand it is classical that the vector space \(\mathfrak m^i/\mathfrak m^{i+1}\) is canonically identified with the \(i\)-th tensor power \(\bigotimes_k^i(\mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2)\) and consequently the vector space \(\operatorname{Hom}_k(\mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2,\mathfrak m^r/\mathfrak m^{r+1})\) is identified, by the homomorphism of "contraction with the \((r-1)\)-th tensor power" \(\bigotimes_k^{r-1}(\mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2)\):
\[ \gamma:\operatorname{Hom}_k(\mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2, \mathfrak m^r/\mathfrak m^{r+1})\longrightarrow \bigotimes_k^{r-1}(\mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2). \]

Via the isomorphism \(\gamma\) the structure of \(G\)-module of \(\operatorname{Hom}_k(\mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2,\mathfrak m^r/\mathfrak m^{r+1})\) is transported to the structure of \(G\)-module of \(\bigotimes_k^{r-1}(\mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2)\) induced by the structure of \(G\)-module of \(\mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2\):
We shall denote by \(\mu(f'_R)\) the image of \(u(f'_R)\) under the isomorphism \(\gamma\).
We can now conclude the proof of lemma 4.7. First of all, the vector space \(\mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2\) being of dimension 1, we have

and consequently \(\operatorname{Im}(G\longrightarrow \operatorname{Aut}_k(\mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2))\) is a finite subgroup of \(k^*\), cyclic, generated by a primitive \(c\)-th root of unity. Denote by \(\varepsilon\) such a root. The subgroup \(\operatorname{Im}(G\longrightarrow\operatorname{Aut}_k(\mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2))\) is therefore generated by the automorphism
The element \(\mu(f'_R)\) of \(\bigotimes_k^{r-1}(\mathfrak m/\mathfrak m^2)\) being invariant under \(G\), we have:
Since \(\mu(f'_R)\) is different from zero (Cond. I), it follows that \(1-\varepsilon^{r-1}=0\), that is, \(c\mid(r-1)\).
Proof of proposition 4.6. We have already remarked that we can reduce to the case \(g=e\), \(\varphi=\operatorname{id}_G\). If we denote by \(G_1\) the kernel of the homomorphism:
then we have, thanks to ramification theory:
Consequently, using the notations of lemma 4.7, we have:

Since on the other hand \(c^{\operatorname{id}_G}(e)=\operatorname{Card}(G)\), the statement of proposition 4.6 in the case \(g=e\), \(\varphi=\operatorname{id}_G\), and consequently in general, follows from lemma 4.7.

blocks.title: Corollary
blocks.html:
Corollary 4.8.

For each \(\ell\ne\operatorname{car}(k)\), the image of the rational number

\[ \frac{\nu(g_R^{-1}f'_R)-1}{c^\varphi(g)} \]

under the canonical inclusion:

\[ \mathbb Q\lhook\joinrel\longrightarrow\mathbb Q_\ell \]

is a \(\ell\)-adic integer.

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 4.9.

Conjecture 4.5 is true if the dimension of the scheme \(X\) is equal to 1.
i) \(\psi(G_{x'})\subset G_{g^{-1}f'x'}\), \(G_{x'}\) being the stabilizer of the point \(x'\).
Consequently we can decompose \(X_x^{\prime\,g^{-1}f'}\) as the union of its orbits, and we obtain:

pages.title: 5. Generalization of a formula of Nielsen-Wecken type. (cf. [1])

blocks.html: 5. Generalization of a formula of Nielsen-Wecken type. (cf. [1])

blocks.title: Proposition
blocks.html:
Proposition 5.1.

The hypotheses and notations being those of 1, 2, 3, 4, suppose moreover that \(Y'\) is a finite subset of \(X'\).

If \(\ell\) is a prime number different from the characteristic of \(k\), we have:

a) the values of the \(\varphi\)-central function

\[ \sum_{x\in Y^f}S_{x,\ell}(f,f',G,\varphi) \]

are \(\ell\)-adic integers.

b) the following formula holds:

\[ (5.1.1)\qquad \operatorname{Tr}_{\mathbb Z_\ell[G],\varphi} (f'_{\mathcal H_{U',X'}^\vee(\ell)}) =\sum_{x\in U^f}\nu_x(f)\operatorname{NW}^{G,\varphi}_f(x) +\sum_{x\in Y^f}S_{x,\ell}(f,f',G,\varphi). \]

Proof. It is clear that if we prove the equality b) considering everything as functions with values in \(\mathbb Q_\ell\), the assertion of a) already follows, because \(\operatorname{NW}^{G,\varphi}_f(x)\), \(\operatorname{Tr}_{\mathbb Z_\ell[G],\varphi} (f'_{\mathcal H_{U',X'}^\vee(\ell)})\) are functions with values in \(\mathbb Z_\ell\).
First, one sees by reducing modulo \(\ell^n\) for each \(n\geq1\) and using (XI, 7.2.3.6) that we have for each \(g\in G\):

\[ (5.2)\qquad c^\varphi(g)\operatorname{Tr}_{\mathbb Z_\ell[G],\varphi} (f'_{\mathcal H_{U',X'}^\vee(\ell)})(g) =\operatorname{Tr}_{\mathbb Z_\ell}(g^{-1}_{X'}f')_{\mathcal H_{U',X'}^\vee(\ell)} =\operatorname{Tr}_{\mathbb Z_\ell}(g^{-1}_{X'}f')_{\mathcal H_{U',X'}(\ell)}. \]
The commutative diagram (1.3), whose rows are exact, gives us (XI, §4):
The two terms of the right-hand side of relation (5.3) can be evaluated thanks to the classical Lefschetz formula (cf. ). (In fact for the last term it is a matter of a trivial Lefschetz formula):

\[ (5.4)\qquad \operatorname{Tr}_{\mathbb Z_\ell}(g^{-1}_{X'}f')_{R\Gamma_{X'}(\mathbb Z_{\ell,X'})} =\sum_{x\in X^f}\ \sum_{x'\in X_x^{\prime\,g^{-1}_{x'}f'}} \nu_{x'}(g^{-1}_{x'}f') \] \[ (5.5)\qquad \operatorname{Tr}_{\mathbb Z_\ell}(g^{-1}_{Y'}f'_{Y'})_{R\Gamma_{Y'}(\mathbb Z_{\ell,Y'})} =\text{number of fixed points of }g^{-1}_{Y'}f'_{Y'}. \]

\[ \begin{aligned} (5.6)\qquad \operatorname{Tr}_{\mathbb Z_\ell}(g^{-1}_{X'}f')_{\mathcal H_{U',X'}(\ell)} ={}&\sum_{x\in U^f}\ \sum_{x'\in X_x^{\prime\,g^{-1}_{x'}f'}} \nu_{x'}(g^{-1}_{x'}f')\\ &-\sum_{x\in Y^f}\ \sum_{x'\in X_x^{\prime\,g^{-1}_{x'}f'}} (1-\nu_{x'}(g^{-1}_{x'}f')) \end{aligned} \]
But \(x\in U^f\) being given, the set \(X_x^{\prime\,g^{-1}_{x'}f'}\) is non-empty if and only if \(\operatorname{NW}^{G,\varphi}_f(x)=1\). Since we also have:
that is to say, the formula to be proved.
5.10. Particular cases. a) The unramified case: \(Y'=\varnothing\), that is \(Y=\varnothing\).
b) The case of multiplicities 1: \(\nu_{x'}(g^{-1}_{X'}f')=1\), \(\forall x'\in Y'^{\,g^{-1}_{Y'}f'_{Y'}}\) and \(\forall g\in G\).
In this case formula 5.1.1 reduces to formula 5.11. This case is in particular realized if we have: \(\dim X=1\), \(\nu_x(f)=1\) for every \(y\in Y\).
6. Application to a Lefschetz formula.

6.1. We shall keep the notations and conditions of section 1x). Let moreover \(A\) be a commutative noetherian \(\Lambda_n\)-algebra, \(F\) a constructible sheaf (for the étale topology) of perfect \(A\)-modules on \(X\) and
We obtain in particular an endomorphism of the pair \((X,F)\):
and consequently an endomorphism of complexes of \(A\)-modules:

The scheme \(X\) being by hypothesis proper over \(k\) and the sheaf \(F\) being constructible and of torsion, the finiteness theorem (S.G.A. 4, XVII 5) assures us that the complex \(R\Gamma_X(F)\) is a perfect complex of \(A\)-modules. The trace \(\operatorname{Tr}_A(R\Gamma_X(u,f))\) is therefore defined
Our aim will be to give, under some supplementary hypotheses, an explicit formula for \(\operatorname{Tr}_A(R\Gamma_X(u,f))\) in terms of local invariants associated to the fixed points of \(f\).
6.2. Definition of the local terms. Suppose that \(X'\) is irreducible of dimension one and that the restriction of the sheaf \(F'=p^*(F)\) to \(U'\) is constant.
If the number of fixed points of \(f'\) is finite, a hypothesis which will be made in the sequel, we may always suppose that we have \(X^f\subset Y^f\).
The sheaf \(F'|_{U'}\) is thus defined by an \(A\)-module \(M\) on which \(G\) acts:
In fact if we denote by \(\eta\) (resp. \(\eta'\)) the generic point of \(X\) (resp. \(X'\)) and by \(\bar\eta\) (resp. \(\bar\eta'\)) a geometric point over \(\eta\) (resp. \(\eta'\)) we have:
The complex \(F'\) is isomorphic to \(M\) and consequently \(M\) is a perfect \(A\)-module.

On the other hand, if the morphism \(f'\) is not constant it leaves fixed the generic point of \(X'\). Consequently the morphism \((p^*(u),f')\) of the pair \((X',F')\) induces a morphism on the stalk \(F'_{\bar\eta'}\):
One sees easily that the following relation is satisfied
\[ (6.2.1)\qquad u_M(\varphi(g)m)=g u_M(m), \quad\forall m\in M,\quad\forall g\in G. \]
The dual \(\check M\) of \(M\) with respect to \(A\) is thus endowed with a structure of (A[G])-module and with an endomorphism
\[ u_{\check M}(gx)=\varphi(g)u_{\check M}(x), \quad\forall g\in G,\quad\forall x\in\check M. \]
Moreover \(\check M\) is a perfect \(A\)-module and consequently the trace \(\operatorname{Tr}_A(g^{-1}u_{\check M})\) is defined for each \(g\in G\).

Let \(x\in X^f\) be a closed fixed point of \(f\). The morphism \(u\) induces an endomorphism of the module \(F_x\), the stalk of the sheaf \(F\) at the point \(x\). (We identify the closed points of \(X\) with the points of \(X(k)\)):
The trace \(\operatorname{Tr}_A(u_x)\) furnishes us a first local invariant associated to the fixed point \(x\).
We introduce another local invariant \(\alpha_x(f,u,f',G,\varphi)\), an element of the ring \(A\), by the formula:
is obtained from the function \(i_\ell\circ S_x(f,f',G,\varphi)\) (cf. 4) by reduction modulo \(\ell^n\mathbb Z_\ell\), that is:

blocks.title: 6.3. A Lefschetz formula.
blocks.html:
6.3. A Lefschetz formula.

Under the hypotheses and with the notations of 5.1, 6.1, 6.2, we have the formula:

\[ (6.3.1)\qquad \operatorname{Tr}_A(R\Gamma_X(u,f)) =\sum_{x\in X^f}\tau_x(f,u,f',G,\varphi), \]

where we have set

\[ (6.3.2)\qquad \tau_x(f,u,f',G,\varphi)=\operatorname{Tr}_A(u_x) -\alpha_x(f,u,f',G,\varphi). \]
Proof. The subscheme \(Y\) being stable under \(f\), the morphism
The last term of formula (6.3.4) can be made explicit thanks to the elementary Lefschetz formula and to the fact that we may always suppose \(X^f=Y^f\):

\[ (6.3.6)\qquad R\Gamma_X((u,f)_{U,X})\simeq R\Gamma^G R\Gamma_{X'}(u',f')_{U',X'},\qquad (u'=p^*(u)), \] \[ (6.3.7)\qquad F_{U',X'}\simeq \Lambda_{n,U',X'}\mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{\Lambda_n}M, \quad (u',f')_{U',X'}=f'\!{}^*_{\Lambda_{n,U',X'}} \mathbin{\overset L\otimes}_{\Lambda_n}u_M, \]
whence, taking into account the general formula (cf. Exp. XI, 7.5.9):

But the trace \(\operatorname{Tr}_{\Lambda_n[G],\varphi} (f'\!{}^*_{H^\vee_{\Lambda_n,U',X'}})\) is computed by means of the Nielsen-Wecken formula (cf. Proposition 5.1.b)), of course reducing modulo \(\ell^n\mathbb Z\).
Taking into account the relation \(X^f=Y^f\), we obtain:
Combining relations 6.3.12, 6.3.11, 6.3.5, 6.3.4 we obtain the formula of the statement.
a) It is easy to see that if \(g\in G\), then we have the relation:
Relation 6.4.1. allows us always to reduce, replacing if necessary \(f'\) by \(g^{-1}f'\) and \(\varphi\) by \(\varphi_g\), to the case where there exists at least one point \(x'\in X_x^{f'}\).
b) Let \(x'=X_x^{f'}\) and let \(G_{x'}\) be the stabilizer of the point \(x'\). Evidently \(G_{x'}\) is stable under \(\varphi\) and we thus obtain an endomorphism

Denote by \(X'/G_{x'}\) the quotient scheme obtained from \(X'\) by the equivalence relation defined by \(G_{x'}\), by \(F'/G_{x'}\) the endomorphism of \(X'/G_{x'}\) obtained from the endomorphism \(f'\) of \(X'\) by the same equivalence relation, and by \(x'\) the class of the point \(x'\) modulo this equivalence relation.
Taking the inverse image under \(p_2\) of the homomorphism \(u\) we obtain a homomorphism

blocks.title: Lemma 6.4.5.
blocks.html:
Lemma 6.4.5. If the following relations hold:
\[ \nu_{x'}(f')>1, \] \[ \nu_{x'}(g^{-1}f')>1, \]

then \(g\) belongs to the first ramification group \(G_1\) (cf. 4.7):

\[ g\in G_1, \]

that is, \(g\) induces the identity on the vector space \(\mathfrak m_{x'}/\mathfrak m_{x'}^2\).

where \(\mathfrak m\) is the maximal ideal of the ring \(k[[T]]\).

In this case the local term \(\tau_x(f,u,f',G,\varphi)\) is given by the formula:

pages.title: 7. Comments on the conditions of validity of the Lefschetz formula.

blocks.html: 7. Comments on the conditions of validity of the Lefschetz formula.
Of course, in practice, one starts with a scheme \(X\) proper over \(k\) and an endomorphism
— \(F\) is a constructible sheaf of \(A\)-modules,
— the set \(X^f\) of fixed points of \(f\) is finite.
The problem is to define under these conditions local terms \(\tau_x(u,f)\), \(x\in X^f\) such that

Using the two duality theorems, global and local, Verdier succeeds in defining the terms \(\tau_x(u,f)\) such that formula (*) is true (cf. III). Unfortunately the explicit determination of the \(\tau_x(u,f)\), defined by Verdier, in terms of known local invariants, seems difficult, whence the interest of the definitions proposed in the preceding chapter.(*)

On the other hand these latter definitions presuppose supplementary hypotheses on \(F\), \(f\) and \(u\), namely the existence of \(U,U',G,f',\varphi\) having the properties required in 5.1, 6.1, 6.2.

blocks.title: Proposition 7.2.
blocks.html:
Proposition 7.2. Let \(X\) be a smooth scheme of dimension 1, \(F\) constructible and
\[ (u,f):(X,F)\longrightarrow(X,F) \]

an endomorphism such that the morphism of sheaves

\[ u:f^*(F)\longrightarrow F \]

is an isomorphism.

Under these conditions there exists \(U,U',G,f',\varphi\) satisfying the conditions of 5.1, 6.1, 6.2.
Proof. Let \(U\) be the largest open subset of \(X\) such that \(F\) is locally constant on \(U\), and denote by \(Y\) its complement. The set \(Y\) is stable under \(f\):

Indeed, suppose by contradiction that there exists \(y\in Y\) such that \(f(y)\in U\). The sheaf \(f^*(F)\) is then locally constant in a neighborhood of \(y\), and using the isomorphism \(u\) it follows that \(F\) is locally constant in a neighborhood of \(y\), which is absurd.

If \(f\) is constant the proof of the proposition is trivial. We shall therefore suppose that \(f\) is not constant. If \(\eta\) is the generic point of \(X\) we shall therefore have \(f(\eta)=\eta\).
Let \(i_\eta:\operatorname{Spec}(k(\eta))\longrightarrow X\) be the canonical inclusion, \(F_\eta=i_\eta^*(F)\) and
the morphism (in fact the isomorphism) of sheaves induced by \(u\), \(f_\eta\) being of course the restriction of \(f\) to \(\operatorname{Spec}(k(\eta))\).
where \(\overline F_\eta\) has a structure of \(H\)-module and the following relation is satisfied:

If \(H_1\) is the largest normal subgroup of \(H\) of finite index such that \(\overline F_\eta^{H_1}=\overline F_\eta\), we have \(\psi(H_1)=H_1\). Such a subgroup exists because the sheaf \(F_\eta\) is constructible.
Let \(K\supset k(\eta)\) be the Galois extension of \(k(\eta)\) corresponding to the subgroup \(H_1\) and \(X'\) the normalization of \(X\) in \(K\). Evidently we can define an endomorphism
such that the system \(U,U',f',G,\varphi\) satisfies the required conditions.
